I spend more time thinking about hair than the average man, I suspect. Partly, that’s because I’m a men’s grooming writer, and I have boxes full of shampoos, [beard oils](https://www.gq.com/story/best-beard-oil), [pomades](https://www.gq.com/story/best-hair-pomade), conditioners, and [co-washes](https://www.gq.com/story/co-washing-is-the-better-way-to-wash-your-hair) and partly it's because I give a lot of thought to the [best shampoos](https://www.gq.com/story/best-shampoo-for-men-for-every-type-of-hair). I would go to war for [Baxter of California’s Deep Clean](https://cna.st/affiliate-) apple cider vinegar shampoo, and I use [Harry’s for dandruff,](https://cna.st/affiliate-) and [Geologie Co-Wash](https://cna.st/affiliate-) for moisture, but I've been curious about Prose shampoo, as well.

You may know the start-up custom hair care, turned skin care line from its Instagram ads that tout different combinations of its hair oil, pre-shampoo scalp masks, dry shampoo, curl creams, and leave-in conditioners as ways to solve key hair needs. The system of products are meant to work with your hair texture to give you good hair days on repeat—minus a lot of the ingredients that folks want to side-step these days including sulfates, parabens, GMOs, phthalates, mineral oils, and silicone (plus its also dye-free and cruelty-free).

If it’s your first time hearing about Prose, getting a set of custom skin care and hair care isn’t really all that daunting. You simply start by taking a questionnaire about how your hair feels (the oiliness, the hydration, whether it’s frizzy, etc.). Then, you answer a few other questions like your fragrance preferences and your hair goals to develop your curation of Prose products. The products show up at your door step. You lather up. And repeat.

To help give you an honest review about whether the skin care and hair care products are worth it, I tried them for a month. Here's my take on what you should get now and what you can skip.

* * *

## Prose Custom Hair Care Review

- [![Image may contain: Bottle, Lotion, Cosmetics, and Perfume](https://media.gq.com/photos/66e1c5f09fa26a3b9ff1cca8/3:4/w_748%2Cc_limit/proe-custom-haircare.png)](https://cna.)

- [![Image may contain: Food, Seasoning, Syrup, Cabinet, and Furniture](https://media.gq.com/photos/66e1c7c37ae12d96b5203bfd/3:4/w_748%2Cc_limit/IMG_2671.jpg)](https://cna.)

- [![Image may contain: Bottle](https://media.gq.com/photos/66e1c7c49595512e32b2badb/3:4/w_748%2Cc_limit/IMG_3538.jpg)](https://cna.)

Prose

### Personalized Haircare Set

[Shop at\\
\\
Prose](https://cna.)

Pros & Cons

AccordionItemContainerButton
LargeChevron

Pros

- Excellent personalization and product packaging
- Wide selection of fresh-smelling fragrances
- Fragrance-free options also available
- Almost immediate effects from some of the hair products

Cons

- More expensive than other haircare lines

If you’re worried that Prose’s personalization is a gimmick—don’t. It’s the real deal. With both the skin care and hair care product lines, I was impressed with the degree of personalization. Prose has no off-the-shelf products; you have to order a custom formula. The initial “consultation” hits a sweet spot: It feels in-depth without feeling like homework. It asks all the questions you’d expect—hair type, age, length, dry/oily roots, split ends, etc.—as well as some questions you wouldn't. Prose uses your ZIP Code to take into account the weather and pollution in your area, as well as how often you need to use shampoo and your overall hair goals. (If you don’t have hair goals, Prose probably isn’t for you, babe.)

As a former hipster, current Brooklyn resident, and elder millennial, I’m a sucker for vibey aesthetics and cool product packaging. Beauty is a virtue unto itself, and this is another area where Prose excels. The product packaging doesn’t contain any unnecessary plastic, and it’s very satisfying to see the name “Timothy” on the product labels. My biggest gripe is that the hair care products don’t include directions. That’s fine for the shampoo and conditioner, but I had to look up directions for the hair care mask online.

Prose

### Custom Hair Mask

[$48\\
Prose](https://cna.)

Let’s just get this out of the way: Prose makes a damn fine shampoo and conditioner. The effects were immediate. Based on the online hair consultation, Prose said that my products were made to enhance shine and extend color. My hair felt different after two or three washes, and after weeks of using it, I experienced less frizz and more shine, as promised. My hair also felt luxuriously soft. Too soft, to be honest. If I had longer hair, or if I were a woman, I think I’d genuinely love these products but given that I need my hair to mold itself as needed, I found this to be tough.

Of the three products that I tried, the conditioner was my favorite, but the shampoo also impressed me. I was able to use it less than most other shampoos, and my hair always felt cleaner and less greasy the day after. Of the three, I found the mask to be slightly confusing to use and time-consuming to properly apply, so it would be my option to skip if you were choosing.

## Prose Custom Skin Care Review

- [![Image may contain: Bottle, and Tape](https://media.gq.com/photos/66e1ce1a7ae12d96b5203c01/3:4/w_748%2Cc_limit/fallback-introduction-section-desktop.jpg)](https://cna.)

- [![Image may contain: Bottle, Cylinder, and Tape](https://media.gq.com/photos/66e1d09c9fa26a3b9ff1ccb0/3:4/w_748%2Cc_limit/IMG_2896.jpg)](https://cna.)

Prose

### Prose Custom Skin Care Subscription

[Shop at\\
\\
Prose](https://cna.)

Pros & Cons

AccordionItemContainerButton
LargeChevron

Pros

- Excellent personalization and product packaging
- Wide selection of fresh-smelling fragrances
- Fragrance-free options also available
- Almost immediate effects from some of the skin products

Cons

- Skin care set doesn’t include a retinol

I genuinely loved the Prose facial cleanser, and it’s the product I most strongly recommend ordering a la carte if you don’t want to commit to a subscription. I also had a good experience with the moisturizer and serum, and overall. I’ve tested a lot of skin care lines in my career, including the customized skin care offering from Geologie, and Prose definitely has a more pared-back approach with a very edited selection of three products: a moisturizer, serum, and face wash.

While the face wash was the clear standout, I had a good experience with the entire regimen. I would've liked the chance to spring for an undereye serum, but I think three products is the right amount for a subscription service. This was my main problem with Geologie, Prose’s biggest competition for custom men’s skin care; Geologie’s set included way too much product for me.

Prose Custom Cleanser

- [![Image may contain: Bottle, Cosmetics, and Perfume](https://media.gq.com/photos/66e1d09e7ae12d96b5203c07/3:4/w_748%2Cc_limit/IMG_3011.jpg)](https://cna.)

Prose

### Custom Cleanser

[$29\\
Prose](https://cna.)

This gel cleanser helped whisk away all of the acne-causing stuff that usually lurks on my skin without overly drying it out. It feels refreshing, and I chose a nice citrusy scent, though I normally avoid skin care products with fragrances, but I’m hooked on the lime-forward Pacifica scent, which also has notes of lavender, lemongrass, and cypress. The fragrance is definitely an area where Prose excels. Plus, folks with sensitive skin and noses can choose fragrance-free options, another win for personalization.

[9 Tasty High-Protein Snacks That Aren't Eggs](https://www.gq.com/story/9-tasty-high-protein-snacks-that-arent-eggs-3#intcid=_gq-right-rail_7bd6cdc5-7521-4ae8-be15-f51db9fa0a1f_popular4-2)







By Tom Ward


The Prose moisturizer was another standout for me. If you appreciate “a little goes a long way” type products, then I think you’ll like it, too. Just a dab or two was enough, and I learned quickly not to overapply this one. Based on my skin care profile, “Timothy’s Moisturizer” includes niacinamide, squalene, fermented rice, lime extract, and red algae extract. My serum was hyaluronic acid-based, with added botanicals.

The serum was my least favorite of the trio, but I enjoyed it as well. I didn’t love the consistency, which was a little too milky for me, but I did like the effects. I do wish one of these products contained retinol, although the serum does have bakuchiol, which Prose describes as a “natural, gentle alternative to retinol.” Personally, I say, give me the retinol!

Final thoughts: Is Prose worth it?

So, would I pay for Prose? Those who are ready to upgrade from drugstore shampoo and conditioner should definitely try Prose, which has earned its reputation in the hair care space. You can also try it for a month before signing up for a subscription, which makes it a low-risk investment.

I think the custom skin care set would be a smart investment for anyone who takes their skin care routine seriously (but not _too_ seriously). Add in [face sunscreen with at least SPF 30](https://www.gq.com/story/best-face-sunscreen), and you’ve got everything you need to keep your skin looking its best.

Prose is on the pricier side compared to drugstore offerings. Seasonal promotions and subscription discounts can bring the price of the haircare subscription to $58; you can get the skin care set for $73 if you catch all the available discounts. I’d recommend starting with a more expensive one-time order and then signing up for a subscription if you see changes to your skin. Plus, you could probably skip every third month or so, as Prose sends subscribers products every four weeks that we'd be able to make last longer.
